Marketing work for B2B and technology products

This portfolio is a collection of projects I've worked on, along with a few self-initiated pieces created to explore product marketing, website messaging, and long-form content for B2B technology companies.

CHAPTER 02

Featured Project

SignalGrid — a concept B2B SaaS platform for industrial equipment monitoring, built to demonstrate product marketing thinking for a deep-tech audience.

SignalGrid

A fictional industrial IoT monitoring platform, built end-to-end as a positioning and messaging exercise: identifying the customer problem, structuring the page, and writing benefit-led copy for an operations audience.

I created the product messaging, information architecture, UX copy, and overall concept. AI helped generate the front-end code — the positioning, copy, content hierarchy, and product narrative are mine.

Positioning UX copy Information architecture B2B SaaS

CHAPTER 03

Writing Samples

Two pieces on technology and product strategy — one on market trends, the other on communicating complex products through customer outcomes.

Opinion · Technology adoption & product strategy

Why Every Company Suddenly Wants an AI Strategy (Even When They Don't Need One)

On why businesses have started competing on perception as much as features — and how to tell real AI value from market pressure.

Read full piece

Over the past year, "AI-powered" has quietly become one of the most overused phrases in tech.

Open almost any software company's website and you'll find AI somewhere on the homepage. Customer support is AI-powered. Analytics is AI-powered. Email is AI-powered. Sometimes it's difficult to tell whether AI is actually solving a problem or simply being mentioned because everyone else is talking about it.

What's interesting isn't the technology itself. It's how quickly businesses have changed the way they talk about their products.

A few years ago, companies competed on features. Faster software. Better pricing. More integrations.

Today, many compete on perception.

Investors ask about AI strategy. Customers expect AI features. Competitors announce AI roadmaps. Before long, companies feel pressured to respond, even if they're still figuring out where AI genuinely fits into their product.

That doesn't mean AI is just hype.

Far from it.

Some of the most useful applications are surprisingly ordinary. Automating repetitive work. Summarising documents. Detecting unusual patterns in large datasets. Helping teams spend less time on manual tasks and more time solving actual problems.

Those use cases may not make headlines, but they're often where businesses see the greatest value.

The challenge is knowing the difference between using AI because it creates measurable value and using it because the market expects to hear the word.

Technology trends come and go.

The companies that benefit the most usually aren't the first to adopt every new tool. They're the ones that ask a much simpler question: does this solve a real problem for our customers?

Because in the end, customers don't buy AI. They buy products that make their work easier.

Business & technology · Communicating complex products

The Best Technology Is Usually Invisible

On why the strongest product marketing rarely starts with specifications, and always starts with the problem.

Read full piece

Every few months, a new technology becomes the centre of attention.

AI. Quantum computing. Digital twins. Edge computing.

The headlines focus on what the technology can do, but the companies that succeed usually tell a different story. They spend far less time talking about the technology itself and far more time talking about the problem it solves.

Think about GPS.

Most people don't think about satellites, atomic clocks, or signal processing when they open Google Maps. They think about getting home without sitting in traffic.

Cloud storage isn't successful because distributed computing is fascinating. It's successful because people don't have to worry about losing their files.

The best technology fades into the background.

That's especially true in B2B companies.

An operations manager doesn't wake up wanting predictive analytics. They want fewer production delays. A hospital doesn't invest in cybersecurity because encryption is exciting. It invests because patient data has to stay protected.

The technology matters.

But the outcome matters more.

That's why some of the strongest product marketing doesn't begin with specifications or architecture diagrams. It begins with a simple question: what's frustrating today, and how can we make it easier tomorrow?

Once people understand the problem, they're much more willing to understand the technology behind the solution.

In many ways, that's what good communication is. Not making technology simpler. Making it more relevant.
